name: Umm Habiba bint Abi Sufyan
title: Umm Habiba bint Abi Sufyan (RA): Royalty Redefined
meta_description: The story of Umm Habiba bint Abi Sufyan (RA), who held firm to her faith through exile and loss, married the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, and chose worship over the power her family later held.
keywords: Umm Habiba bint Abi Sufyan, story of Umm Habiba, Abu Sufyan daughter, mothers of the believers, wife of Prophet Muhammad, sahaba stories
lede: She had left Makkah a young woman and stayed away for thirteen years. In that time she lost her livelihood, lost her husband, raised a daughter alone in a foreign land, and watched her own father become the chief enemy of the faith she had chosen. At any point, she could have walked back into comfort. She never did.

This is the second part of the story of Umm Habiba bint Abi Sufyan (may Allah be pleased with her): a woman whose steadiness through exile was answered with marriage to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, and who, when real power finally came within reach, quietly turned away from it.

sections.h2: A faith that would not bend
paras: Before we follow her home, it is worth pausing on what she had already endured. She became Muslim in Makkah and faced the anger of Abu Sufyan and her family for it. When the call came to migrate, she did not hold back, even though her position could have shielded her from persecution if she had stayed.

She kept her faith through the long migration to Abyssinia. She kept it when her husband, Ubaydullah ibn Jahsh, reportedly left Islam and then died there. She kept it while her father led the opposition against the Prophet ﷺ back home. And she kept it through years of exile, alone with her young daughter, far from everything familiar.
There were many doors out of her faith, and she walked through none of them. She only grew firmer. This was a woman whose istiqamah, whose steadiness, was tested from every side, and held.

Her marriage contract to the Prophet ﷺ had been written while she was still in Abyssinia, but six years passed before they would actually meet as husband and wife. In that long wait, her daughter Habiba grew up before her eyes.

At last the time came. Seven years after the migration, in the calm that followed the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, Jafar ibn Abi Talib (may Allah be pleased with him) led a delegation of sixteen families from Abyssinia to Madinah. Now there was safety on the road, and joy in the destination.

For Umm Habiba the joy was layered. She had been away from her land, her family, and her home for thirteen years. She had sacrificed her livelihood, lost her husband, and had a child in exile. And now she would meet the Prophet ﷺ again, this time not only as a believer, but as his wife.

In Madinah she was reunited with Umm Salama (may Allah be pleased with her), with whom she shared a deep friendship. Their lives had run in parallel: both had left Makkah for Abyssinia with their husbands, both had children there, both were widowed, and both, by very different paths, came to be wives of the Prophet ﷺ.

They would sit and reminisce about Abyssinia together, and describe it to the Prophet ﷺ. In one narration carried by Aisha (may Allah be pleased with her), Umm Habiba and Umm Salama told him about a church they had seen there, with its images and tombs.

He warned them about a people who, when a righteous person among them died, would build a place of worship over the grave and fill it with such images. Those, he said, would be among the worst of people before Allah. In that single hadith, three of the most prolific narrators of the Prophet ﷺ are gathered: Aisha, Umm Salama, and Umm Habiba.

There was a tenderness in Umm Habiba that shows in another moment. She once asked the Prophet ﷺ whether he might also marry her sister. Surprised, he asked if that was truly what she wanted, and she said she would be glad for her own sister to share in this honour. He told her gently that it was not lawful for him to marry two sisters. Even where Allah had widened the rules of marriage for the Prophet ﷺ alone, the prohibition on marrying sisters still stood, and he kept to it.

After the Makkans broke the treaty, Abu Sufyan came to Madinah to try to repair it. He went to the Prophet ﷺ, who rose and walked away. He went to Abu Bakr, then Umar, then Fatima (may Allah be pleased with them). Every door closed. At last he came to the home of his own daughter, whom he had not seen in years.

Inside, Umm Habiba was sitting on the Prophet's simple bed, a palm mattress with a single blanket, used as a couch by day. As her father moved to sit beside her on it, she quietly folded the mattress away and let him sit on the floor.

He asked her whether the bed was too good for him, or he too good for the bed. She answered plainly: this was the bed of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, and he was not worthy to sit where the Prophet ﷺ sat. She would not let the man who still opposed her faith share that place. The doors had closed on Abu Sufyan completely.

It is worth noticing what kind of place she was guarding. The home of the most honoured man in Arabia held a palm mattress and one blanket. Her father, a chief used to comfort and rank, found in the Prophet's house neither, and a daughter who would not trade her faith for his approval.

sections.h2: A long prostration for her family
paras: When Makkah was finally opened, the Prophet ﷺ met its people not with revenge but with mercy. He gave a general amnesty to those who had spent decades fighting him, sparing only a few who posed an active danger. He even gave Abu Sufyan a measure of honour, declaring that whoever entered his house would be safe. This was never about ego or revenge. It was about the mission, and about leaving every door open for people to change.

It was then that Abu Sufyan, and her brother Muawiyah, accepted Islam. Umm Habiba had never stopped loving her family or longing for their faith. It is said that the two longest prostrations of her life were the day her father accepted Islam and the day her brother did. She wanted for them exactly what she had wanted for herself.

Once the Prophet ﷺ found her making a tender supplication, asking Allah for a long life in the company of her husband, her father, and her brother. Gently, he taught her something deeper: that lifespans and provisions are already fixed, and that she would do better to ask Allah for protection from the punishment of the grave and the Fire. He turned her hope away from the days of this world and toward the next.
Umm Habiba lived a long life, long enough to see her brother rise and the Umayyad house come to power before her eyes. Privilege was now hers for the taking. She did not take it.

She avoided politics. She avoided the civil strife of her time. She kept clear of the trials that pulled others in, and gave her years instead to worship, to teaching, and to carrying the words of the Prophet ﷺ. When her family rose to the height of worldly power, she stepped quietly in the other direction.

Yet standing apart from the strife did not make her cold to suffering. When Uthman (may Allah be pleased with him) was besieged and even denied water, she rode out on a mule with water hidden under her garment, hoping her standing might let her reach him. A young man blocked her, snatched the water and threw it to the ground, then cut the reins so the mule bolted and she fell before everyone. She used what privilege she had not for herself, but to help a man being wronged.

When she was dying, she called for Aisha and asked forgiveness for any small disputes that had passed between them, the ordinary friction of shared lives. Aisha forgave her and asked Allah to release her from any account. Umm Habiba smiled and said, You have made me happy, may Allah make you happy. Then she sent for Umm Salama and said the same. Three of the great mothers of the believers, settling every account before she went.

Umm Habiba narrated sixty-five hadith of the Prophet ﷺ, ten in Bukhari and eight in Muslim. Her brothers learned from her, her daughter Habiba learned from her, and students like Zaynab bint Umm Salama and Urwa ibn al-Zubayr carried her narrations forward.

Among them is one of the best loved practices in Islam. She narrated that the Prophet ﷺ said whoever prays twelve units of voluntary prayer in a day and night, Allah will build for them a house in Paradise. The way she taught it is unforgettable. I never left those twelve, she said, since I heard them from the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. Each narrator after her added the same vow: I never left them since I heard them.

It was not a chain of words passed along. It was a chain of people who lived what they learned. Those twelve units, two before Fajr, four before and two after Dhuhr, two after Maghrib and two after Isha, reach us today through her.

lessons_heading: What Umm Habiba's life teaches us
lessons_intro: Her story is not built on a single moment. It is built on holding a position, year after year, when no one would have blamed her for letting go.

lessons.bold: Steadiness is its own kind of strength.
lessons.text: Door after door opened back toward comfort and safety. She kept her faith through all of them, and only grew firmer for it.

lessons.bold: Love your family and still hold your line.
lessons.text: She longed for her father's faith and rejoiced when it came, yet she would not let his opposition seat him in a place that was not his.
When her own family reached the height of worldly power, she turned toward worship and teaching instead, and avoided the trials that pulled others in.
She did not just pass on the reward of twelve units of prayer. She never once left them, and that is why the practice still reaches us.

faq.q: Who was Umm Habiba bint Abi Sufyan?
faq.a: An early Muslim and one of the Mothers of the Believers. She was the daughter of Abu Sufyan, migrated to Abyssinia for her faith, and later married the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. She became one of the most important female narrators of hadith.

faq.q: Why did she refuse to let her father sit on the bed?
faq.a: At that time Abu Sufyan was still leading the opposition to the Prophet ﷺ. When he came to her home in Madinah, she folded away the Prophet's mattress so he could not sit on it, telling him it was the Messenger's place and he was not worthy of it.

faq.q: What is Umm Habiba best known for narrating?
faq.a: She narrated the hadith that whoever prays twelve voluntary units in a day and night, Allah will build them a house in Paradise: two before Fajr, four before and two after Dhuhr, two after Maghrib, and two after Isha.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Umm Habiba?
faq.a: Steadfastness through long hardship, loving your family without compromising your faith, choosing worship over worldly power, and living what you learn rather than only repeating it.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us are never tested by a single dramatic choice. We are tested slowly, by the option to quietly let go: of a habit we know is right, of a boundary that has become inconvenient, of a faith that would be easier to set down. Umm Habiba was offered that exit again and again, across thirteen years and then across a lifetime, and she kept choosing to hold.

Her life asks a gentle question of an ordinary day. When ease pulls one way and conviction the other, which do we follow? She loved her family and still kept her line. She had every reason to chase status and chose worship instead. Carry even a little of that steadiness into today, and her story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Umm Habiba (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute), the second part of her story. Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
She left Makkah a young woman.

She stayed away thirteen years.

In that time she lost her livelihood, her husband, and watched her own father lead the fight against her faith.

She never walked back.

Her husband died in exile. Her father became the chief enemy of Islam.

Door after door opened back toward comfort.

Umm Habiba (RA) walked through none of them.

After six years of waiting, she came home to Madinah, not only as a believer, but as the wife of the Prophet ﷺ.

Thirteen years away. Now reunited with her old friend Umm Salama.

Her father, Abu Sufyan, came to her home, still opposing the faith.

He moved to sit beside her on the Prophet's simple bed.

She quietly folded the mattress away and let him sit on the floor.

"Is the bed too good for me, or am I too good for it?"

"This is the bed of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. You are not worthy to sit where he sits."

She loved her father. She would not move her line.

When her family finally accepted Islam, it is said her two longest prostrations were the day her father believed and the day her brother believed.

She had wanted this for them all along.

Her family rose to the height of worldly power.

Umm Habiba turned the other way: no politics, no privilege.

She gave her life to worship and to teaching instead.

**Live what you learn.**

She taught that twelve units of prayer build a house in Paradise, then said: I never once left them.

Those prayers reach us through her.

May Allah be pleased with her.
social.caption:
Umm Habiba bint Abi Sufyan (may Allah be pleased with her) spent thirteen years in exile for her faith. She lost her livelihood and her husband, and watched her own father lead the opposition to the Prophet ﷺ.

At every turn there was a door back to comfort. She never took it. When her father came to her home in Madinah, still her faith's opponent, she would not even let him sit on the Prophet's simple bed.

Later, when her family reached the height of worldly power, she turned away from it and gave her life to worship and teaching. The twelve units of daily prayer, the ones the Prophet ﷺ said build a house in Paradise, reach us through her.

Which part of her story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: She had every reason to walk away, and never did
email.alt_subject: "This is the bed of the Messenger of Allah"
email.preview: Thirteen years in exile, a father who opposed her faith, and a steadiness that never broke.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Some people are tested by one dramatic moment. Umm Habiba bint Abi Sufyan (may Allah be pleased with her) was tested slowly, for years, by the simple option to quietly let go.

She became Muslim in Makkah and faced her family's anger for it. When the call to migrate came, she did not hold back, though her standing could have protected her if she had stayed. She kept her faith through the long exile in Abyssinia. She kept it when her husband died there. She kept it while her own father led the opposition to the Prophet ﷺ back home.

Thirteen years she was away, alone with her young daughter, far from everything familiar. There were many doors back to comfort. She walked through none of them. She only grew firmer.

Her steadiness was answered. In the peace after Hudaybiyyah she came home to Madinah, not only as a believer but as the wife of the Prophet ﷺ. There she was reunited with her old friend Umm Salama, and the two would sit and remember their years in exile.

Then her father came to her door, still opposing the faith, hoping to use her. She was sitting on the Prophet's simple bed, a palm mattress with a single blanket. As he moved to sit beside her, she quietly folded the mattress away and let him sit on the floor. This is the bed of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, she told him, and you are not worthy to sit where he sits. She loved her father, and longed for the day he would believe. But while he opposed the truth, she would not move her line.

Years later, her family rose to the height of worldly power. Umm Habiba turned the other way. No politics, no privilege. She gave her life to worship and to teaching instead.

There is a lesson in her that is easy to miss: faith is often kept not in one grand stand, but in the quiet refusal to let go. When ease pulls one way and conviction the other, she kept choosing to hold.

May Allah be pleased with her, and grant us a measure of her steadiness.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: She had left Makkah a young woman and stayed away for thirteen years. In that time she lost her livelihood, lost her husband, raised a daughter alone in a foreign land, and watched her own father become the chief enemy of the faith she had chosen.

Umm Habiba bint Abi Sufyan (may Allah be pleased with her) kept her faith through all of it. She kept it when she migrated to Abyssinia. She kept it when her husband died there. She kept it while her father led the opposition back home. There were many doors out, and she walked through none of them. She only grew firmer.

In the calm after Hudaybiyyah, she came home at last, not only as a believer but as the wife of the Prophet ﷺ. In Madinah she was reunited with her old friend Umm Salama, and the two would sit and remember their years in exile together.

Then her father came to her door. Abu Sufyan still opposed the faith, and every other door in Madinah had closed on him. She was sitting on the Prophet's simple bed, a palm mattress with one blanket. As her father moved to sit beside her, she quietly folded the mattress away and let him sit on the floor.

He asked whether the bed was too good for him, or he too good for it. This is the bed of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, she answered, and you are not worthy to sit where he sits. She loved her father, and longed for his faith. It is said that one of the two longest prostrations of her life was the day he finally accepted Islam. But while he opposed the truth, she would not move her line.

She lived a long life, long enough to see her own family rise to the height of worldly power. She wanted none of it. She avoided politics and privilege and the strife of her time, and gave her years to worship and to teaching instead.

Through her comes one of the best loved practices in Islam: that whoever prays twelve voluntary units in a day and night, Allah will build them a house in Paradise. She did not only pass on the reward. I never left those twelve, she said, since I heard them from the Messenger of Allah ﷺ.
storybook.pullquote: This is the bed of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. You are not worthy to sit where he sits.
storybook.reflection: Umm Habiba was offered an easy way out again and again, and kept choosing to hold. She loved her family and still kept her faith. She had every reason to chase status and chose worship instead. Most of us are tested the same quiet way: not by one great moment, but by the daily option to let go of what we know is right.
storybook.source: Her story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
